docs.title: Applied AI Engineering Handbook
docs.text:
This handbook documents my journey toward becoming a production-ready Applied AI Engineer.

Topics include:

  • Backend Engineering
  • AI Systems
  • RAG
  • Cloud Infrastructure
  • Docker
  • GCP
  • Software Architecture
  • AI Agents
  • Production Systems

docs.title: Layer 1 — Software Engineering Foundations
docs.text:
• Python Engineering
  • Git & GitHub
  • Project Structure
  • Software Architecture

docs.title: Layer 2 — Backend Engineering
docs.text:
• HTTP
  • APIs
  • Authentication
  • Databases
  • Business Logic

docs.title: Layer 3 — AI Engineering
docs.text:
• LLMs
  • RAG
  • Vector Databases
  • Agents

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Become a production-ready Applied AI Engineer with strong backend, cloud, and AI systems knowledge.

docs.title: Authentication & JWT
docs.text: Authentication and authorization are core concepts in backend engineering and production AI systems.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Authentication answers:

Who are you?

Examples:

  • Login
  • Email/password verification
  • Identity verification

docs.title: Authorization
docs.text:
Authorization answers:

What are you allowed to do?

Examples:

  • Can access premium AI model?
  • Has enough quota?
  • Is admin?
  • Enterprise user?

docs.title: Detailed View
docs.text:
flowchart TD

A[Step 1 - Login]
--> B[Step 2 - Server Verifies]

B --> C[Check Email and Password]

C --> D[Authenticated User]

D --> E[Step 3 - Server Creates Token]

E --> F[Step 4 - Client Stores Token]

F --> G[Step 5 - Every Request Includes Token]

G --> H[Backend Verifies Token]

H --> I[Allow or Deny Request]

docs.title: Helicopter View
docs.text:
flowchart LR

A[Browser / Frontend]
--> B[Send Email + Password Once]

B --> C[Backend]

C --> D[JWT Token]

D --> A

docs.title: Why Do We Need Tokens?
docs.text:
HTTP is stateless.

Each request is independent.

The server does not automatically remember previous requests.

Tokens help applications identify users across multiple requests.

docs.title: 1. Random Tokens
docs.text:
Example:

x7A91P0Q

The server stores token-related information inside a database or session store.

docs.title: 2. JWT (JSON Web Token)
docs.text:
JWT stores information directly inside the token payload.

Example:

xxxxx.yyyyy.zzzzz

Payload example:

{
  "user_id": 7,
  "name": "Ahmed",
  "role": "admin"
}

docs.title: JWT Structure
docs.text:
JWT contains 3 parts:

HEADER.PAYLOAD.SIGNATURE

docs.title: Header
docs.text:
Contains token metadata.

Example:

{
  "alg": "HS256",
  "typ": "JWT"
}

docs.title: Payload
docs.text:
Contains user-related information.

Example:

{
  "user_id": 7,
  "role": "admin",
  "exp": 1712345678
}

docs.title: Signature
docs.text:
The server signs the token using a secret key.

Conceptually:

SIGN(header + payload, secret_key)

This prevents unauthorized modifications.

docs.title: Why JWT Is Powerful
docs.text:
The server does not need to store authentication session state.

The token itself contains the information.

This enables scalable authentication systems.

docs.title: JWT Security
docs.text:
JWT is usually signed, not encrypted.

Anyone can read the payload, but they should not be able to modify it.

Never store:

  • passwords
  • API secrets
  • highly sensitive data

inside JWT payloads.

docs.title: Sessions vs JWT
docs.text:
flowchart LR

A[Client]
--> B[Server]

B --> C[(Session Store)]

C --> B

In session-based authentication:

  • the server stores session state
  • the backend remembers users
  • scaling becomes harder

docs.title: JWT Stateless Authentication
docs.text:
flowchart LR

A[Client + JWT]
--> B[Server A]

A --> C[Server B]

A --> D[Server C]

In JWT authentication:

  • any server can validate the token
  • authentication becomes easier to scale
  • no centralized session store is required

docs.title: What Is State?
docs.text:
State = information that must persist between requests.

Example:

Ahmed is logged in

A traditional session-based system stores this information on the server.

This is called a stateful system.

docs.title: The Scaling Problem
docs.text:
In cloud and distributed systems:

  • multiple servers exist
  • requests may hit different servers
  • sessions become harder to synchronize

Example:

Load Balancer
    ↓
Server A
Server B
Server C

docs.title: JWT and Stateless Authentication
docs.text:
JWT enables stateless authentication.

Any server can validate the token independently.

This makes scaling easier in:

  • cloud systems
  • microservices
  • AI APIs
  • serverless environments

docs.title: Important Note
docs.text:
Stateless does NOT mean:

No database exists.

Databases are still used for:

  • users
  • quotas
  • billing
  • refresh tokens
  • revoked tokens

However:

Authentication session state itself does not need to be stored.

docs.title: Request Example
docs.text:
GET /profile
Authorization: Bearer JWT_TOKEN

The backend verifies the token before processing the request.

docs.title: Common HTTP Authentication Status Codes
docs.text: Status Code Meaning 200 Success 401 Unauthorized 403 Forbidden

docs.title: Access Token
docs.text:
Short-lived token used for API requests.

Example:

  • 15 minutes
  • 1 hour

docs.title: Refresh Token
docs.text: Used to generate new access tokens without logging in again.

docs.title: Why This Matters for AI Systems
docs.text:
Modern AI systems rely heavily on:

  • APIs
  • authentication
  • permissions
  • quotas
  • scalable cloud infrastructure

Example:

POST /chat

The backend needs to determine:

  • who the user is
  • whether they are allowed to access the AI system
  • quota limits
  • enterprise permissions

JWT is commonly used to solve this problem in production systems.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• HTTP is stateless
  • Tokens help identify users
  • JWT stores data inside the token payload
  • JWT enables scalable authentication systems
  • Authentication and authorization are different concepts
  • Stateless authentication is important for cloud-native systems
  • AI systems rely heavily on authentication and API security

docs.title: What is HTTP?
docs.text:
HTTP = HyperText Transfer Protocol

It is the communication protocol between:

  • Frontend
  • Backend
  • APIs
  • Browsers
  • AI services

docs.title: Why HTTP Matters
docs.text:
Modern applications communicate using HTTP.

Example:

Frontend → Backend Mobile App → API AI Service → Backend

docs.title: HTTP is Stateless
docs.text:
Each request is independent.

The server does not automatically remember previous requests.

docs.title: GET
docs.text:
Retrieve data.

Example:

GET /users

docs.title: POST
docs.text:
Send data.

Example:

POST /chat

docs.title: PUT / PATCH
docs.text: Update data.

docs.title: DELETE
docs.text: Delete data.

docs.title: Request Structure
docs.text:
An HTTP request usually contains:

  • Method
  • URL
  • Headers
  • Body

Example:

Authorization: Bearer JWT_TOKEN

docs.title: Response Structure
docs.text:
A response usually contains:

  • Status code
  • Headers
  • JSON data

Example:

{ "message": "Success" }

docs.title: 200 OK
docs.text: Success.

docs.title: 201 Created
docs.text: Resource created successfully.

docs.title: 400 Bad Request
docs.text: Client error.

docs.title: 401 Unauthorized
docs.text: Authentication required.

docs.title: 403 Forbidden
docs.text: Authenticated but not allowed.

docs.title: 404 Not Found
docs.text: Resource not found.

docs.title: 500 Internal Server Error
docs.text: Backend/server issue.

docs.title: Why HTTP Matters for AI Systems
docs.text:
AI applications rely heavily on APIs and HTTP communication.

Examples:

  • Chat APIs
  • Embedding APIs
  • Authentication services
  • AI gateways

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: HTTP is stateless.

docs.title: Traditional Solution
docs.text: Sessions.

docs.title: Problem with Sessions
docs.text: Hard to scale in cloud systems.

docs.title: Modern Solution
docs.text: JWT.

docs.title: Why JWT is Stateless
docs.text: The server does not store authentication session state. The token itself contains the information.

docs.title: Why This Matters for AI Systems
docs.text: AI APIs need scalable authentication systems.
